candidates.stmt:
Dr Munir Abbasi is Labour’s candidate for Central Greenford in the 2026 London Borough of Ealing council elections. A proud resident of Greenford, he is committed to serving the local community and ensuring that residents’ voices are represented in local decision-making using bottom-up approach.

Dr Abbasi holds a PhD in engineering and is a professional engineer. His work focuses on delivering practical solutions that support resilient communities, social innovation, advance climate-neutral, and sustainable development.

Alongside his professional career, Dr Abbasi has been actively involved in community service. He is serving as a school governor, trustee of the hub@Greenford Library, and previously sat on the Ealing Citizens’ Equality Tribunal, helping to address inequality and promote fairness across the borough.
He is passionate about strong public services, safer neighbourhoods, cleaner streets and parks, opportunities for young people, and fair development that benefits local residents.
 Dr Abbasi is committed to working with the community to help make Central Greenford a cleaner, safer, and more prosperous place for everyone.

I am honoured to be standing as the Green Party candidate for Central Greenford ward. I have always been passionate about a political system that works for everyone, and have consistently spoken out against discrimination throughout my life and work. Through my day job, where I champion diversity and inclusion, and my role as a volunteer paramedic, I have always strived to promote kindness and care in everything I do. I joined the Green Party in 2023, after being inspired by their commitment to social justice and meaningful environmental change.

Having lived in West London my whole life, I understand how important our local community is and the role it plays in people’s everyday lives. If elected, I would work to ensure that everyone in our community is supported and protected – from the most vulnerable to those whose voices too often go unheard.

I would champion the rights of immigrants – who are such a vital part of our communities – fight to protect and expand our green spaces, and advocate for affordable, safe housing. I would also support the campaign to save Ealing Hospital, and work closely with residents to ensure their voices are heard and reflected in local decision-making.

Frank has lived in the London Borough of Ealing for 25 years and, before his recent retirement, was an economist running a research and consulting business in the borough. His work on the parcels and delivery industry was frequently cited in national newspapers.

He joined the Green Party in 2010, drawn by its commitment to building an economy that works for people in a sustainable way.

Frank has been active in local politics and campaigning for many years and is a governor at a local school. He was a co-founder of West Ealing Neighbours, a community group supporting engagement in local issues.

He lives in Ealing with his wife and young daughter. In his spare time he enjoys cycling and is interested in practical ways to improve local transport, public spaces and quality of life across the borough.

My message is simple – the current government is not working in your best interest. Our NHS is in peril, ambulances take far too long to reach emergency cases, police forces have been so budget-starved that they have had to rely on known sex offenders within their ranks. This is just the start of the list of problems with the current government. 

Unfortunately the system at the moment doesn’t work. Our democracy is broken, because it almost always boils down to a choice between Conservative and Labour, both of which have very similar manifestos and both of which are currently committed to keeping this broken system. I firmly value the idea of democratic representation. That means that I want your votes to actually determine the amount of power a party has.

I have lived on the same road in Central Greenford since 1982. My family and I are part of this community, and I am standing as an independent candidate to give residents a genuine local voice — one that works for people, not a political party.

I was educated locally, attending Ealing College Upper School, and went on to complete a BSc, MPH and PhD while continuing to live in Central Greenford. I later spent several years conducting research between the UK and the USA. I now work as an independent research scientist and serve as a school governor. These experiences shape my approach to local issues, grounded in evidence‑based decision‑making, fairness, and public service.

These are the issues I believe matter most to residents:

1. Cleaner, safer streets
Tackling litter, fly-tipping, and local environmental issues to keep Central Greenford clean and well maintained.

2. Fair parking and traffic enforcement
Ensuring parking rules are applied fairly and consistently, with proper consideration of residents’ circumstances.

3. Better local services
Pushing for reliable, responsive services that residents can depend on — from waste collection to street maintenance.

4. Fairness in housing and planning
Supporting transparent decisions and developments that meet local needs.

5. Public health and wellbeing
Supporting practical local measures that reduce alcohol and gambling harm and improve community wellbeing, including stronger controls on advertising and a focus on prevention.

I have already worked to improve road safety on Oldfield Lane North, surveying the area with a road-safety expert and calling for a zebra crossing near the Clare Road roundabout — a campaign I will continue to pursue.

As an independent councillor, I will listen, act, and stand up for Central Greenford residents every time. I will be accessible and available to residents, with regular opportunities to raise concerns directly.

Raj has lived in Ealing for 39 years and worked in the transport logistics, manufacturing and security industries. He has been a union rep for two unions. Raj has supported local campaigns, concluding those to save accident and emergency services and keep the maternity unit open at Ealing hospital, keep Southall Town Hall open and keep a factory open in North Ealing that employs disabled people. He has previously stood as a council candidate for Ealing in Hanger hill, and as a parliamentary candidate in Hammersmith and Chiswick. Living in the area with his family, Raj understands the everyday concerns facing residents. He is standing to represent our community with integrity, listen to your concerns, and work collaboratively to deliver real improvements.

READY FOR POSITIVE CHANGE

Local voice for local view's - Vote for an Independent Candidate.

Dear neighbour's and residents,  My name is Khalil and I am a dedicated local resident deeply invested in our community's well-being. With three decades of experience in education and a commitment to service, Khalil serves as a school governor, NHS volunteer, and currently, as a University Lecturer. His passion for education, community, economy, healthcare, environment, and public services drives his vision for a brighter future. Khalil is committed to investing in our community, fostering safety, health, and prosperity for all, as we work together to build a stronger, more vibrant community.

Campaign  Commitments  1. To improve local roads, tackle potholes, and ensure cleaner streets for safer journeys and a tidier community.

2. Focus on enhancing transport links and bus services to make commuting easier and more reliable for all residents.

3. To Prioritise the enhancement of local healthcare services and public amenities, ensuring better access and quality of care for our community.

4. Support the regeneration of our local area, we will invest in the local economy and prioritise the development of workforce skills to create jobs and opportunities.

5. Engaging with young people is essential. We will provide improved services, expand educational and sports facilities, and revitalise youth clubs to empower and support our future generations.

I joined the Green Party because it offers something that no other party does – hope. The hope for a better life for everyone and everything that lives on the planet.
In the 20 years I’ve called Ealing home, I’ve fought to make our borough greener and better. The hardest fight was to save Warren Farm. As one of the core campaign team, we fought for five years to save this precious habitat from Labour’s development plans. I’ve worked with CURB (Clean Up The River Brent) to improve our local waterway, protected a secret bluebell wood, and campaigned to make the urban environment better too – from School Streets to Clean Air campaigning.
To steal a quote from Green Party Leader Zack Polanski, there is no environmental justice without social justice. I believe that we live in a time of great inequality. Having raised a family while working several jobs – landscape gardener, decorator, handyman, writer – I know the struggle to pay bills as prices soar. And the anger at seeing the divide between the average worker and the super-rich widen yet again. The Green Party are the only party with plans to change this unfairness. This local election is a chance to be part of that change.

Adam is a local secondary school teacher. He sits on Ealing Broadway's Safer Neighbourhoods Police Panel, and is a member of Central Ealing Residents Asssociation (CERA). He is also an active member of The Questors Theatre - a community theatre right in the heart of Ealing.

Seema has raised her family in Ealing and dedicated 35 years to charitable and voluntary work. As an Ealing Broadway Councillor Seema's priorities are working to ensure the neighbourhood is kept clean and safe, that troublemakers are stopped and that our heritage is left intact. She believes people should be able to live in a pleasant environment, and she's always there for any resident who needs help.

Francesco is a lifelong Ealing resident. He has lived here his whole life, raised his family here, and dedicated years to local organisations, from Age UK Ealing to the Pitshanger Area Forum. Francesco is also a member of the Pitshanger Police Panel. Francesco has previously stood for the Liberal Democrats on several occasions, including the 2016 Ealing & Hillingdon London Assembly elections.

Mandy has lived in the borough for 25 years and raised two children here, who went to St Benedict's Junior School. She has been team leader at the volunteer-run Northfields Community Library for 5.5 years, in addition to volunteering for many local organisations including The Store Cupboard, HomeStart, Volunteer Help and at the Winter Night Shelter.
https://ealing.greenparty.org.uk/2026-local-election/ealing-broadway/

I have worked as a teacher in London schools for over 20 years, and have lived in the borough since 2004. I am the current co-ordinator of the Ealing Green party and have campaigned to save Ealing’s open spaces, including Warren Farm, from development. Ealing is my home and I believe that getting Greens elected to the local council can make it an even better place to live.

A Voice for Ealing: Walking Marky
For 41 years, Ealing has been my home. Many know me as “Walking Marky,” and you may have seen my advocacy on social media where I have been campaigning tirelessly to highlight that our cycle lanes are constantly full of cars. Our streets must be safe for everyone—especially our children and cyclists.
My journey began by fighting for safer conditions outside the Infant School at the Grove. Since then, I have successfully reported drivers for over 1,000 traffic offences across West Ealing and Ealing Broadway. My work proves that we can all make a difference if we just put in a little bit of effort; local vigilance creates real change.
I stand for transparency. I successfully fought a Community Protection Notice used to stifle my safety reports, and I will always defend our right to speak out against incompetence. As your Green Party candidate, I will ensure our streets are safe and that your voice is the priority.
My Pledges:
Zero Tolerance, Positive Enforcement: Strict action against those who park or drive dangerously around our schools.
Clear Cycle Lanes: Ensuring infrastructure is used correctly, not for illegal parking.
Accountability: A Council that finally listens and acts.
Vote Walking Marky. A neighbour already doing the work.

Iram Woolley calls Ealing her home, and has lived in Ealing for 25 years. Passionate about improving the life chances for our children across Ealing, she is a former School Governor, and Ealing Schools. She has served as GMB London sisters Chair and Sectetary collectively working on workers rights.

She has served as Campaign Coordinator. She is dedicated to serving her community, and is also a former volunteer, at the Felix Project, as well as part of the 50:50 Parliament.

She was also a Labour BiteSize Facilitator. If elected, Iram will fight to help reduce poverty, inequality, and pollution, by improving services and creating new opportunities, for all residents, especially children, young people, and those from protected characteristics.

Jon Ball has been a community campaigner in Ealing Common for 25 years. He has been elected as a councillor for the Ealing Common ward since 2002. He enjoys helping local people and working with local groups campaigning on key issues for our community like protecting services at our local hospitals.

Jon successfully battled to save the Solace Centre for people with mental illness from the Labour-controlled council’s closure plan. He has fought for affordable housing and against inappropriate developments such as a 40-storey skyscraper in central Ealing.

A passionate pro-European, Jon campaigned hard to remain in Europe in the referendum and is still fighting against a damaging, hard Brexit.

Jon has a young son and is concerned about the air pollution implications for the area of a third runway at Heathrow airport.

Outside politics Jon works in the film and TV industry.

I have been a resident in Ealing borough for 30 years and have been a councillor in Ealing Common ward science May 2022. It was Brexit which made me join the Liberal Democrats and become locally active in Ealing Common ward. Connecting with residents and fighting for our borough is my service. My role in the Lib Dem opposition team is focussed around safety and community, working with the police and the safer neighbourhood team to increase safety in our ward.
Lakhbir Singh, former Mayor of Luton, regularly uncovers and reports fly tips in Ealing Common. He also volunteers for LAGER Can, to keep our streets and parks clean.

I have extensive experience in the community and charity sector, with a passion for building more welcoming and supportive communities. I lead a charity that helps refugees rebuild their lives through nature-based and cultural activities. I also work for a crisis support charity, supporting victims of domestic abuse and sexual violence.

I believe decisions should be made at the community level, wherever possible. Local people are best placed to understand community needs, and should be given the power to shape their own future.

I’ve lived in Ealing for over 20 years and always taken a keen interest in the community. I served as a school governor for 8 years, where as Chair of Resources I helped keep the school budget balanced and ensure that we provided the children with excellent teaching and support. I was also a trustee and Treasurer of the school PTA, a reading support volunteer, and over Covid was a volunteer in a local vaccination centre.
In my work I lead change programmes for a large education charity, and I hope that will bring transferable skills in planning and delivery of improvements.
Ealing is blessed with green spaces which has made it a great place to bring up my family, and should I be elected my priorities would be to protect and nurture our local environment, promote energy efficiency, and with my background in education to ensure that the council delivers on its support for local schools, especially in areas such as SEND.

I’m Rabia Nasimi, a proud Acton resident and one of your Labour candidates for East Acton.

I’ve spent over 15 years in public service and charity work, standing up for people and our community. As a local mum, I’ll be visible and approachable, holding regular surgeries and making sure your voice is heard at the Council.

I’ll be helping Labour deliver safer and greener streets, better local services, and a future where everyone in East Acton can thrive. I’ll be a councillor who listens, acts, and delivers for our community.

I’ve been on the doorstep every week and have really enjoyed talking to local residents. I’d be happy to speak and meet with you if you’re interested.

For 20 years, East Acton has been my home, and I have served as a ward councillor since 2010. I’ve championed new housing, and schools, while standing up for the most vulnerable. My background is in local government and housing policy and means I know how to drive real change. As Mayor of Ealing in 2023, I supported local Acton charities including EASE and APPLE (in Acton Park).

I know the value of communication and connection with residents and communities and will always support local groups looking to improve the lives of the most vulnerable in our community and across the Borough.

My name is Sam and I am the Treasurer of Ealing Green Party.

I moved to Ealing whilst working for the BBC as a sound technician before going freelance.

I have been a member of Ealing Green Party for over 30 years making me the longest serving member of the party!

I have lived in the borough of Ealing since 1987 in both Hanwell and Chiswick.

In that time I have seen Ealing Greens go from strength to strength. At the last local elections, of Ealing’s twenty-four wards, we were second place in Hanwell Broadway and South Acton, and the third place party (ahead of the LibDems) in sixteen. In Westminster elections, we more than doubled our vote count and won third place in Ealing Southall and Ealing North. We feel close to getting Green Party representation on Ealing Council.

I am a criminal justice practitioner and community advocate based in West London. Committed to social justice, public safety and community empowerment.

I grew up in Ealing with strong ties to Acton, where I made my first home. I attended local schools, St Gregory’s and St Augustine’s Priory and have fond memories of Ealing Cricket Club where my father played in the first team—shaping my deep connection to the community.

My work brings together grassroots organising, partnership working and neighbourhood engagement. I have supported community justice initiatives and served on the advisory board of Tell MAMA helping to tackle hate.

I am the founder and voluntary owner of Ealing Freegle, which I have run since 2010 as part of the national Freegle network, supporting 24,000 Ealing Borough subscribers through reuse and mutual aid.

I am particularly committed to tackling anti-social behaviour (ASB) and unlicensed HMOs which impact neighbourhood safety. Residents face noise nuisance, alcohol and drug-related activity and intimidation linked to poorly managed properties while vulnerable tenants are often exploited.

I will be a visible, accessible councillor working alongside residents to deliver safer streets, better housing and stronger communities.

I’m standing as the Green Party candidate for East Acton in the May 2026 local elections because I believe ordinary people – not career politicians – deserve a stronger voice.

After nearly 19 years in Ealing, I’ve seen poor council decisions: threats to Warren Farm, overdevelopment without affordable housing, neglected green spaces, and failure to support residents affected by projects like HS2.

This must change. Ealing should work for everyone, and more Green councillors will mean real collaboration with residents.

I’ve previously stood in local and London Assembly elections and bring experience in community organising, climate activism, citizens’ assemblies, and social justice work. I cycle everywhere, support a local animal rescue, and work in nature conservation. In my professional life I have managed a variety of teams, worked in media and press teams, community-based projects and campaigning, mostly under high pressure and chaos – I’d say I am ready to face the duties of a councillor with confidence.

I want to bring transparency and accountability to Ealing, as well as listening to residents’ needs and concerns and building a plan together to improve lives, neighbourhoods and communities.

Abdi Ahmed is a lifelong Ealing Borough resident. He works for a Liberal Democrat Member of Parliament as a Parliamentary Researcher, focusing on issues such as local government finance, public services, and cost-of-living pressures. Abdi previously worked on a £48 million UK Foreign Office programme supporting greener, more resilient cities. He is a fluent Somali speaker, able to engage and represent East Acton's diverse communities, and is experienced in dealing with policy casework in Parliament, helping residents get answers and solutions.

Benedict Cross has lived in Ealing Borough all his life. He volunteers at Ealing Oxfam Bookshop and Ealing Sanctuary Hub, assisting refugees in East Acton ward. Benedict is a law graduate and veteran campaigner, excited to serve as Councillor.

I am standing as a candidate for Ealing because I believe local residents deserve a council that is accountable, transparent, and focused on delivering real results. As a parent with children in local schools, I am deeply invested in our community and understand the importance of safe neighbourhoods, strong education, and reliable public services.

I want to ensure that council decisions reflect the needs of residents, that public money is spent responsibly, and that your voice is heard. I will work hard to bring practical, common-sense solutions that improve everyday life in Ealing.

My family has called Ealing home since the 1930s, and I’ve proudly lived here since 1990. Having run small businesses and households on tight budgets, I know how to make every penny count. I will work with residents and businesses to restore Ealing as the Queen of the Suburbs — tackling crime, ensuring cleaner streets, standing up for drivers, controlling HMOs, and cutting the waste of our money.

I am standing for the Green Party as a candidate for Greenford Broadway.

I decided to become a more active supporter the local Green Party in Ealing after seeing their work campaigning against children’s centre closures and for the protection of our important green spaces.

As a legal aid lawyer for many years, I saw the impact of decades of austerity, the hostile environment for migrants, poor air quality and unaffordable, unfit housing. I believe in the power of local communities coming together to make a change. One of the
ways to do that is through our local councillors.

As a councillor my priorities would be cleaner air, safer travel and protecting community and green spaces.

I’m standing as a Green Party candidate because I believe Ealing should be a fairer, greener place for everyone. I’ve lived in Ealing for 15 years, and it’s a fantastic community. As a local resident and parent, I care deeply about the future of our borough and the opportunities available to the next generation.

I’m really excited by the recent surge in support for the Green Party locally. Support has been steadily growing over the years, and more and more people want to see practical action on issues like affordable housing, tackling inequality, and protecting our environment.

I’m also the election agent for our local campaign – the person responsible for making sure the campaign is run properly and follows election rules.

There are lots of ways to get involved, whether that’s helping deliver leaflets, talking to neighbours while canvassing, supporting on social media, or simply coming along to local events. Every bit of help makes a difference.

Sean is a local resident in the London Borough of Ealing. He lived in Ealing his whole life and was educated locally in the borough. He left for University in Oxford and returned back to Ealing afterwards. He currently works for the John Lewis Partnership in Waitrose Supply Chain.

Cathy grew up in Hayes and Ealing. She left London for work in the 1970s and returned to the borough after she retired. A keen environmentalist, Cathy was dismayed by the rubbish spoiling our open spaces. Since 2020 she has led the Litter Action Group for Ealing Residents (LAGER Can), which donates labour equivalent to that of ten full-time employees per year. Cathy believes littering and flytipping could be significantly reduced if facilities were available that made it as cheap and easy as possible for residents to dispose of their waste responsibly.

Volunteering all over the borough has heightened Cathy’s understanding of other major issues that negatively affect residents, including the development of property for investment rather than homes, lack of policing and increased antisocial behaviour, loss of amentities, and transport policies. Cathy is keen to work hard to ensure that residents’ concerns are heard and their needs are addressed.

Yousef is a Palestinian‑British civil engineer and environmental scientist who has lived in Ealing for more than 25 years. Born in 1953, he brings decades of technical expertise in sustainable infrastructure and community-focused planning. His long-standing commitment to Greenford Broadway is shaped by a belief in fairness, cleaner streets, safer neighbourhoods, and practical solutions that improve daily life. With deep local roots and a career built on evidence-based problem‑solving, Yousef is dedicated to ensuring residents’ voices shape the future of Ealing.

I am a Londoner who has lived and raised a family in Ealing, moving into my first property more than 25 years ago. I bring a wealth of private and public sector experience which can be put to work as a Councillor. I was a Main Board Director and interim Chief Executive at Ofcom – the UK’s telecoms, postal and media regulator - where I was responsible for competition policy and enforcement. Prior to that I was a Partner at PwC where I led international teams delivering projects for global companies in the technology and telecoms sectors.

I am committed to making Ealing the best Borough in London for everyone in our community.

Mark has been a school governor at Ravenor School for 11 years. For over a decade, he has been a volunteer and Trustee at Hounslow Foodbox, and for the last seven years, he has served as treasurer of Friends of The Victoria Hall.

As someone who proudly lives in Hanger Hill ward, I’m standing as a Conservative candidate for Hanger Hill because this community is my home and its future matters deeply to me. As a young and eager candidate, I bring fresh energy, new ideas, and a strong commitment to making a positive difference for local residents.
Living in the ward means I experience the same local challenges and opportunities as my neighbours. I understand the issues that matter most to our community, whether that’s: local services, housing, safety, or maintaining a clean and welcoming environment and I am motivated to work hard to address them.
I want to ensure that every resident feels heard and represented, and I am determined to be a strong voice for Hanger Hill. My goal is to help build a community that works for everyone, now and in the future.

I am standing as a Green Candidate to represent the Hanger Hill ward, so I can represent the community that’s been my home for nearly 20 years.

Councils face huge challenges in stretching budgets to cover everything residents need. To do this as well as possible, I strongly believe communication with residents about the issues and difficult decisions is essential. I’m proud to be part of Ealing Green Party, who have worked hard to listen to residents’ concerns, to challenge decisions and push for action.

In my job as a palliative care doctor, I’m used to communicating difficult things, and to working with both health and social care systems to get the right care for people. I’ve seen the impact of poor housing on health, and of a struggling care system.

As chair of a local Residents Association, I have a good understanding of the issues around crime and antisocial behaviour. I’d continue to work on addressing these, making sure we all feel safe in our homes and on our streets.

I’m a school governor at a local school, and see the amazing things achieved with tight budgets. I’d continue to listen to what our schools need and advocate for our young people.

We have some wonderful green spaces across Ealing, including in Hanger Hill, which I would ensure are protected and well cared for. These are vital for both physical and mental health.

The climate crisis sadly remains one of the biggest unaddressed issues of our time, which increasingly affects us and will shape our children’s futures. I joined the Green Party to be part of the political changes needed to prioritise this. In Hanger Hill I would work to bring meaningful progress by championing energy efficiency and renewable energy schemes- I’ve been successful in winning some money from a government grant scheme to explore a project in Hanger Hill. I’d work with residents to make the changes we need to help make walking and cycling easier, while making sure changes are made fairly. Hanger Hill particularly suff

Anthony has lived in Ealing all his life. Anthony was born in Ealing, went to school in Ealing and works locally as a veterinary surgeon. He is married with two adult children. Anthony has been a Councillor since 1978, and is particularly interested in the Environment, the state of Ealing's roads and pavements, local parks and open spaces.

My name is Hadi, and I am honoured to be standing as a candidate to represent our community.

Having grown up in Hanger Hill, I have developed a strong appreciation for our area and the people who make it what it is. I have been actively involved in volunteering within our community, helping care for local parks and protect the green spaces that residents value. I also lead weekly food drives in Acton and Westminster, organising volunteers and providing hot food and drinks to those in need. Through this work, I have developed a strong sense of responsibility, teamwork, and a commitment to standing up for those whose voices are often overlooked.

I am passionate about serving our community with integrity, ensuring residents feel represented, and helping to build a fairer, more supportive local area. I believe effective representation begins with listening, and I am committed to being an approachable, reliable, and dedicated voice for our community.

I am currently a Chemical Engineering student and Course Representative, where I work closely with students and staff to ensure voices are heard and meaningful improvements are made. This role has strengthened my commitment to listening, advocating, and delivering practical solutions.

I moved to Ealing three years ago during my training at drama school. Since then, I’ve become very fond of the borough, acting as a bridge for me between the familiarity of my hometown and the enormousnous of London.

I’ve found the community to be welcoming and nurturing; looking out for its neighbours and the wider world. For me, I feel very passionate with the idea that something that brings us together is the arts. Fortunately, I have found that Ealing has a great relationship with the arts, through our local theatre, cinema and galleries. I’ve even had the pleasure of being able to bring theatre to our local primary schools.

I believe the arts can be an absolute champion for good but it is often overlooked, disregarded, or seen as a waste of time. I hope to build on it further in our community and prove that it has a place in everyone’s lives; Not only to entertain but I believe it can create change, build inclusion, and inspire.

Catherine Wallace is an emerita Professor in Language and Literacy Education at UCL/Institute of Education. She has lived in Ealing for over 50 years, working at what is now the University of West London for twenty years. She has been a member of the Labour Party for 50 years.

I have lived in Ealing, London's greenest borough, since 1999. I am a passionate environmentalist and am a member of the Labour Party's Socialist and and Environmental Resources Association. I have recently visited all of the different parts of Hanger Hill ward to talk to local people and to find out about local issues and concerns. I believe that the best interests of Hanger Hill ward, its estates, and its communities, can best be represented by electing three Labour councillors who are part of a majority Labour Group, where, if Labour is re-elected to control Ealing Council, key decisions affecting local life in Hanger Hill ward would be made.

Earlier in my career I served as a councillor in another London borough, so I have useful experience to offer. Before that, I taught English in Japan for a year, so would be interested in working with Hanger Hill's Japanese community on local concerns. To be an elected politician it helps to have hinterland - I speak fluent French and German and am a keen member of Ealing's town twinning group with Marcq-en-Barœul which is a suburb of the French city of Lille. I am a cyclist who loves pedalling along the Grand Union Canal towpath and it's great to see new cycle paths springing up across the borough.

Peter has lived and worked in Ealing & Southall for 30 years now. After graduating with a double-first class degree in law at University of West London, he is now a barrister specialising in human rights law.

A well-known local campaigner, Peter has successfully stopped excessive developments while providing good homes for everyone.

You may recall Peter standing in previous elections for the Green, Renew and Labour Parties where he has always done well.

I have lived in Ealing since 1991 and am active in voluntary and non-profits sectors.

My earlier career comprised three decades in corporate and entrepreneurial business working with large organisations in public and private sectors across logistics, retail and financial services, latterly co-founding a successful professional services firm. I now use my expertise in mainly voluntary and trustee capacities to help turn round struggling non-profit organisations and establish “community building” initiatives, including in my local Catholic parish of Ealing Abbey and a pandemic scheme with other volunteers for large-scale backstop neighbourhood support.

My late wife and I decided to stay in Ealing after marrying in the mid 1990s as a great London environment in which to put down roots. Our parents, Hungarian refugees and from an East African Asian family respectively, equated welcome into the UK with the responsibility to appreciate the culture they were joining, assimilate and contribute to in every way we could. Following the tragic death of my late wife from cancer, I am now remarried and with our grown-up adopted sons together encompass over half a dozen ethnic groups. I have personal experience of bereavement, including tragically from suicide, and I strongly support initiatives associated with such impacts.

I’d be honoured to serve Ealing Central and Acton and am committed to being a community-building constituency MP.

Having grown up in Ealing, Natalia has witnessed first-hand the struggles our community faces - from lack of safe cycling infrastructure to green spaces being under threat of development. She has been an environmental campaigner from a young age and coordinated Covid-19 mutual aid efforts in her local community during the lockdowns. Natalia is committed to ensuring all of Ealing North's constituents, including our young people, have a powerful voice representing them.

I am standing as a Green Party candidate to be one of your councillors in South Acton.

While I have always had an interest in politics, I have never stood before for public office largely because I prioritised other areas of my life. I previously worked in a university and in publishing in the UK and abroad. Since 2014 I have run my own small business providing educational services in English Language Teaching. During this time, I spent several years supporting the care of parents with disabilities and dementia. I also relocated to Ealing which involved a period renting and 18 months ago, I settled in Northfields, where I live with my wife, a therapist working in schools.

All these direct experiences as teacher, businessperson, carer, renter, and ‘migrant’ as well as the experiences of my family have made me increasingly frustrated with the current focus of politics. It’s become about division and how people should serve the economy and growth, rather than the economy serving the fundamentals we all need: a secure home, good health and a thriving environment. I joined the Green Party because it is the only party whose policies start from this perspective.

As a councillor, I will remain focused on these values. For example, I’d like to:

help renters challenge poor practices and get the best deal we can get in new developments, so renters can make a home in Ealing fight for more support for home insulations and renewable energy encourage council use of local businesses and build community wealth support grassroots groups working to develop physical and mental health for all ages work together to improve public spaces and having more people out in the spaces to increase community and security Professionally, I am a writer, teacher and trainer, so I am a good listener and communicator. I am an independent voice, uninterested in petty party politics. I will always work with others who want better homes, health and environment in South Acton. As I now work part-time,

Yoel is Labour's Parliamentary Candidate for Esher and Walton. He has worked in international education for thirty years and has been a Councillor in the London Borough of Ealing for two decades.

Yoel will campaign on the issues that residents in Esher and Walton tell him matter the most to them: the lack of genuinely affordable housing, inadequate funding for local NHS services and the lack of real action on climate change. The Tories and Lib Dems have taken residents for granted for too long - If elected as the Labour MP for Esher and Walton Yoel will fight to represent the interests of local people at Westminster and make sure their voice is heard.

There is nothing more important to me than standing up for everyone in Lady Margaret Ward. Safer, accessible travel, remains a key priority. That’s why I am committed to securing significant investment to repair our streets, fix potholes, and improve pavements.

I am proud of our work to protect council tax exemptions for the most vulnerable, as well as the creation of good, well-paid jobs in Ealing and will always welcome responsible businesses that strengthen our local community.

Hi,

I am Nicholas Chapman. I have lived here since 2008 and love where we live. I work in Regulatory Compliance as a Senior Manager at a bank, and bring experience of finance as well as a love of sustainability and passion for social justice, human rights and fairness.

We want to make Ealing safer, cleaner and more democratic. We believe Ealing Council needs a Green voice so good decisions are made openly and honestly on behalf of residents. Check out our Facebook page, and please get in touch if you would like to talk about any local issues.

I’m a local parent, and my family is what motivates me to get involved and make a difference.

I care about the everyday things that affect people’s lives, from cleaner streets and safer spaces for children to communities that feel supported.

I’ve been getting involved locally through litter picking, environmental activities, and community groups, and I want to do more by continuing to show up and contribute.

Robert has lived in the Lady Margaret ward, Southall all his life. He is fully aware of the local issues like fly-tipping, crime, HMOs, potholes, and youth activities. Robert is a highly experienced professional, having worked on Heathrow Terminal 5, Submarines, Nuclear Power, and many other major transport and infrastructure programmes. Robert fully supports local business and the Heathrow 3rd runway. Robert's main drivers for standing are saving Christ the Redeemer Church from demolition, reducing crime and litter, standing up for motorists, providing great facilities and places for Lady Margaret residents, bringing Southall FC home, and ensuring Ealing Council operates effectively and efficiently.

Simon Anthony was born in Hanwell and lived and worked in Ealing for over twenty years. He started work at 17 as an apprentice with Post Office Telecoms before joining the BBC as a Video Tape engineer. In Nottingham he started a family and became a teacher, programmer, QA software engineer, computer journalist college lecturer and occasional GP election candidate.
In 2004 he met his wife, a public service managerial consultant, online – in Australia. They had to sell their `Nottingham home and flat hop in London multiple times chasing work before moving to Sydney for almost five years. Back in the UK they lived in Barking and stood in the 2024 General Election, coming third just 200 votes behind Reform.

He has been disabled by Long Covid since 2020 but by careful ‘Pacing’ his efforts, he is a professional Santa and actor. They moved to a retirement home in Acton last year.

They have four adult children, two from his ex partner, two from his wife and two grandchildren.
He joined the Green Party in 1991 and has stood many times. He finds the manifesto, incredibly, matches what is in his heart – no really !
Simon is Anti War, Anti Bigotry, Anti Violence of any sort. He is fully Pro Free thinking, Classical music, Sci Fi eg Dr Who, Star Trek etc oh, and environmentalism!

I'm a proud Londoner and North Acton resident. I moved here after studying for an MBA at Cambridge and am now serving the community as a school governor in Ealing. I’m standing to deliver genuinely affordable homes, cleaner streets and real action on crime and antisocial behaviour.

With a background in public policy and product management, I bring a practical, data-driven approach focused on results for residents. I’ll work hard to help deliver the Ealing Labour manifesto for our community.

I’m a disaster scientist. Someone who goes to where the risk is, studies it up close, and works out how to protect people from it. That same drive has taken me into higher education, through a PhD, and into schools across London where I help young people understand the world around them and how to stay safe in it. I push myself hard because I believe the stakes are real.

What I care about

North Acton deserves better. Too little green space, crime well above the national average, choking pollution and a housing crisis with no relief in sight. The growing desperation in our community isn’t an accident. It’s the result of doing nothing and calling it stability, of parties too afraid to act and too comfortable to try. And when people cry out for help, this government’s response is to blame the very people it has failed.

What I’ll do

Nothing changes if we keep accepting it. Our future is under genuine threat and I’ve seen firsthand what happens when communities aren’t prepared. But together we can build something stronger. I’ll push hard for green space, safer streets and real investment in this community. If you’re done waiting for someone to actually mean it, vote Green, vote for North Acton.

I’ve lived in North Acton for 35 years and volunteer to keep it clean and safe—tackling litter, fly-tipping and graffiti - and have been nominated for a citizenship award. Professionally, I specialise in improving public services and digital transformation, and I’ll bring that experience to the council. I will improve services, deliver value for money, and ensure residents are listened to.

I’m proud to be standing in North Greenford as your Green Party candidate. Living locally in Ealing, I’m committed to working with residents to build a fairer, more sustainable community that reflects your priorities.

Local politics should be about listening properly and taking action for cleaner, safer streets, stronger local services, and a council that’s open, responsive, and accountable every month, not just at election time.

Alongside my community focus, I bring experience from the infrastructure and construction industry, where I negotiate contracts and advocate for proper scrutiny of how public funds – raised from your hard‑earned money – are spent. I’ve also led diversity and inclusion work, seeing both the positive impact it can have and how easily progress can be rolled back when the wider political climate shifts. That’s why lasting change must be rooted in communities and carried through with consistency.

Politics works best when it’s grounded in real‑life experience. We need more people to step forward, serve their communities for a period of time, and bring practical skills into decision‑making – rather than treating politics as a lifelong career.

From Sudbury Hill to Horsenden, I’ll stand up for residents across North Greenford all year round – focused on delivering a greener, fairer borough that works for all of us.

Who I am Upbringing - I am originally from ‘Nottingdale’, in North Kensington. I grew up in social housing on the edge of Edward Woods Estate, a stone’s throw from multi-million-pound houses and vast wealth.  Kensington and Chelsea was and still is one of the boroughs with the largest divide between the richest and poorest residents. Disparity like this is something that spurred me to always vote for the Labour Party and eventually join it to campaign for a more equal society for everyone.

Hanwell Resident - I have been proud to call the borough of Ealing home for the last 15 years. The past 9 years of which have been in Hanwell (Hobbayne Ward), where I have been an active member of this vibrant community. Being a local resident, I feel from the offset that I would be a highly visible Labour councillor.  From my door to door canvassing and local volunteering I feel I already have a good understanding and grasp of immediate issues that need to be addressed and dealt with. 

Community support - The Covid19 pandemic has put increased pressure and stress on our local community. The importance of looking after each other and supporting our vulnerable and shielding residents is at the forefront. Throughout the various lockdowns I have volunteered to support our more vulnerable members of the community by running errands and offering support where needed.

Local parent - My son was less than a year old when I took the plunge and joined the Labour Party. As a mother of a young baby I became more determined than ever to want change and create a more equal society with opportunities for all.  He now attends a local school in Hanwell that I am an active participant in.

Campaigning – Right from the start of my membership I became an active local Labour Party member. I have canvassed and campaigned for Local, London and General Election campaigns. This has been both locally and neighbouring constituencies. I have knocked door to door across the Hobbayne Ward listening to the c

Hello, my name is Dominic. I am a candidate for North Hanwell and the fundraising officer for Ealing Green Party.

I have been a member of the Green Party for about 5 years, switching after having grown more and more tired of watching Labour move away from my values, and developed growing concern about our path towards climate catastrophe. The Green Party feels like the only party willing to call out what needs to be done.

I have lived in Hanwell for just over a year and a half, but I absolutely love the community here. In that time, I have started a local Guerrilla gardening group – who I’m proud to say are transforming Hanwell’s green spaces – and spearheaded a campaign against plans for an exploitative 24-hour slots casino in the centre of Hanwell. I’ve also volunteered for ‘LAGER Can’ litter picking group to try and reduce litter in our area, working with the council to have extra bins installed in key areas like Hanwell station.

Although I’m fairly new to Hanwell, I’m all too aware of the issues that face it. If elected, I want to help North Hanwell defeat the blight of HMOs – both for the communities they sit in, but also to help stop vulnerable people being trapped as HMO residents in poor conditions that so often come with them.

I am honoured to be standing as a Green Party candidate to be one of your councillors in North Hanwell ward.

I am, barring the time I spent studying for my Geography degree in Nottingham, a lifelong resident of Northolt. After graduating during the pandemic, I worked in airport security at Heathrow, which helped me develop skills as an effective communicator and taught me how rewarding it was to work with the public, solving problems. I now work as a private tutor for primary and secondary school pupils whilst continuing my own education, and in my spare time I enjoy gardening and foraging, through which I’ve learnt how crucial to our everyday lives protecting our local environment is.

Living in the borough of Ealing has shaped my life and I feel very fortunate to have been raised in such a diverse, beautiful and welcoming community. But I’ve also seen how our borough has suffered in recent years. I’ve long had an interest in politics, but I was driven to become more politically active after witnessing first-hand how soaring rental costs were pricing my generation out of the housing market and forcing young people to leave our community just to be able to afford to start a family. I joined the Green Party in 2025 because, under the leadership of Zack Polanski, the Greens are the only party offering a progressive vision of Britain. One that rejects the politics of hate and distrust threatening to divide our communities, and which stands up for all of us.

As your councillor, I want to focus on the housing crisis in our borough. In particular, I’ll pressure the council to prioritise true affordability, sustainability and the needs of locals over developers’ profits when building new housing. I also want to work on improving access for all to our parks and green spaces, and on delivering improvements in safety and accessibility for walking and cycling.

I have lived in Ealing for nearly 40 years, having brought up a family here and maintained active involvement in green issues. I can build on long experience as a team member in helping to manage voluntary, campaigning and commercial organisations. If I have the honour to be elected, I will serve the needs of my constituents to keep pressure on the council to build an equitable society, ensuring that all facilities especially housing and transport (cycling!) are affordable, healthy and sustainable.  Check out our Facebook page, and please get in touch if you would like to talk about any local issues.

Dear Fellow Residents,

Well, I have lived in Ealing all my life, and in Hanwell Broadway since 1982 onwards. I grew up here, and am a proud Hanwellian. My professional background is in the Rail Industry, I am also a member of the RMT Union, and was a local Health & Safety Rep for my workplace for 7 years. I am a family man, and am a Dad of two teenage children. The last 20 years, I have been a member of the Socialist Party (www.socialistparty.org.uk), who are part of the Trade Unionist & Socialist Coalition/TUSC (www.tusc.org.uk).

My main priorities for the North Hanwell ward is now where Ealing Labour Council has approved a 4.99% rise in Council Tax! Now, where is the money going? And yet our services are chronically underfunded, and the cost of living and austerity continue to affect the lives of our residents!

Local residents across the whole of Ealing borough, including this very North Hanwell ward are still faced with continuous onslaught of vicious austerity cuts since the last 16 years, tearing down our public sector services, and dismantling the fabric of our community structure.

What's the point of a Labour Government and a Labour Council, if they continue the same continuous onslaught of Tory style Austerity Cuts, directly attacking the working class communities? Every council service has faced cuts, job losses and Austerity! Since 2010, under the Tory Rule, Ealing Council has lost 56% of its funding in real terms. Now, Labour are in power in both the Council & Government, but nothing seems to have changed!

In 2024, Labour promised change, but all we got was the same old cruel Tory Policies. They all say "better our cuts than their cuts" and continue to make us all pay for the system's crisis! Fight Back Against Austerity We need Real Anti Austerity Councillors, who will Fight Back Against Austerity Cuts and to stand up to Starmer's Labour Government, and vote for No - Cuts Needs Budgets and Campaign within our communities and trade unions to win back

Nikki has been an Ealing resident all her life, an Ealing teacher for the past 17 years and a mum for the past 10 years. Nikki is proud to be standing in the community in which she lives and loves; there's nothing more special than supporting our local area, particularly our green spaces and vibrant neighbourhoods.

Hi, I’m Ian Douglas and at 79 am probably the oldest member of Ealing Green Party (unlike Sam who is the longest-serving member and much younger than me). In fact, I only joined in 2014 – surprisingly already 12 years ago.
Although I’m a teacher, I’m now semi-retired as I was recently acting as a full-time carer for my wife who died last year.
Ever since studying the ecology section of my geography degree in the late 1960s, I have been very aware of the intimate and often delicate connections between all life forms and their environment. This, of course, includes us humans, who have an unfortunate tendency to cause all manner of imbalances in our environment, leading not only to destruction of local habitats and the extinction of many species, but also to the possible catastrophic effects of climate change and plastic pollution.
In the late 80s, my wife and I became passionate recyclers and were aghast at how slow progress in this field has been in this country. Ealing residents may well be amazed that many local authorities, even in London, don’t provide separate food waste collection. At least that’s one thing we can congratulate Ealing for!
Other than this, I am particularly interested in housing issues (there are far too many empty properties), as well as the worsening scourge of fly-tipping which I believe needs far stronger action.

Nigel Bakhai has lived in the borough for over 25 years including 2 years in Windmill Road in Northfields. He was an Ealing Lib Dem Councillor for Elthorne ward which included part of the current Northfields ward from 2010 to 2014 and gained a reputation as a hard-working and assiduous local councillor dealing with a wide ranging and large volume of casework, in particular obtaining council funding to renovate play areas in local parks and supporting activities for children during school holidays. He has worked in Logistics and Supply Chain roles for 30 years in major companies like Electrolux and Xerox.
Robert Hall has lived in the borough of Ealing for several years. He is a volunteer at “The Connection” which helps rough sleepers get off the streets and build a much better life.

Craig Smith is leader of Ealing Community Independents – a new political party, here to contest the local elections on May 7 and bring fresh ideas to the borough.

For too long, Ealing Council has been run by a single party that consistently fails residents: they break promises, they don't listen, they fail to consult properly, and they cut our public services while increasing council tax.

>>>

Craig has lived in Ealing for more than 25 years – helping bring up two children through local authority schools. A proud community campaigner and trade unionist, he is committed to equality, justice, peace and human rights.

He believes key local decisions should be participatory – shaped with, not done to, residents and workers.

Craig is a founder of the Save Ealing Children’s Centres campaign, a former school governor, and serves as an officer of Ealing Trades Union Council. He is a former business journalist and now strategic communications director, with experience working collaboratively at a senior level within large, matrixed organisations.

>>>

Ealing Community Independents is a people-led local party committed to putting residents’ voices and choices first, in every neighbourhood from Acton to Southall.

We exist to challenge a council culture that answers upward to Westminster parties instead of outward to local people. We will replace it with accountable, independent, community-rooted representatives.

Ealing Community Independents believe the current system is broken and that existing political parties are not doing enough to support everyone in our society.

We will offer the people of Ealing an alternative at the next council elections in May 2026. We will be your voice, your party, representing your choices. We will commit to bring public services, jobs and power back to those who have been neglected.

Ealing Community Independents stand for investment in public services – not cuts, closures or privatisation. We stand for equality and social justice. We wil

Mike has lived in Northfield ward for over 26 years. Now retired, he has worked as a librarian and researcher in the public and private sectors. A veteran campaigner for peace and social justice, he is an officer of Ealing Trades Union Council and the local branch of Unite Community.

Mike joined Ealing Community Independents because he believes Ealing deserves better. A council that listens. Investment in public services – not cuts, closures, or privatisation. Truly affordable homes for all. A council that does not support companies in violation of international law and human rights in Palestine and across the world.

Florence has lived in Ealing for over 30 years and both her children went to school here. She used to work locally and now works for a trade union as an executive assistant. Most of her closest friends live in the borough. This is genuinely Florence’s home, and that’s exactly why she wants to serve it.

Florence has been a trade union representative for nearly two years, and serves as branch secretary at work, which has given her real skills in listening, representing people fairly, and challenging decisions that aren’t in the community’s interest. Her priorities are housing affordability, schools and young people, and putting residents back in charge of decisions that affect their neighbourhood. Too often, people who live in an area have the best ideas about fixing it – but nobody takes the time to ask them. Florence will.

I’m standing for Northolt Mandeville because, like many residents, I’ve been frustrated by the lack of accountability from the Labour council. Through my career as a data analyst, I’ve developed strong problem solving skills and experience delivering complex projects. I want to bring a fresh voice to the council with a focus on delivering real results for local people.

I have been an Ealing resident for 35 years and am now looking to put my skills and experience to use for the benefit of the community. 

I am a recent entrant into active politics as I have come to recognise that it is not sufficient to criticise others. If I believe there is a better way of doing politics then I need to be willing to actively participate. I hope to be given the opportunity to do so.

It has been an honour for Fabio to serve the community, whether working with residents to have deterrent signs for HGVs installed, working with businesses and police to gate off a fly-tipping hotspot or working with colleagues and parents to improve a playground. Fabio is an NHS doctor who cares deeply about health issues. He is particularly passionate about championing the Motor Neuron Disease Charter.

Mark joined Ealing Community Independents to try and make a difference. Mark has always lived within the Borough and has lived in Southall for over 30 years.

Mark is a civil servant and has been a Customs Officer in the past protecting our borders. He is an active disabled trade union campaigner for better public services and was part of the save Ealing tax office campaign before it eventually moved to Stratford.

Mark is a stroke survivor so was involved in the local Trades Council campaign to stop Ealing hospital A&E being downgraded to an Urgent Care Centre.

Born and raised in Southall, Happy has lived, worked, and served in the community that shaped him. His life is guided by duty, justice, and a commitment to defending the rights of his neighbours. Southall is his lifelong home, and his goal is to give back to the place he loves while helping preserve the values that strengthen England. Known for his integrity and independence, he cannot be bought or influenced – his loyalty is to the people alone. He stands for honour, dignity, respect, and love for everyone who calls Southall home.

Jahangir is a technology professional with over 25 years’ experience in technology, research, and enterprise leadership. He currently leads analytics and digital transformation projects within an international organisation. He has also served in policy and non-profit roles supporting sectoral development and child education initiatives.  He is standing in Norwood Green because he believes stronger local leadership is needed to prevent the rise of far right driven by wrong Labour policies and their overall poor economic performance. His priorities would also be about addressing deteriorating living standards in the ward and tackling youth joblessness. He wants to revitalise Southall’s local businesses and create real employment pathways for residents in a meaningful way.

Debbie is a lifelong West Londoner and award‑winning community advocate who has called Perivale home for over 25 years. With professional experience in education, local government and the NHS, she understands the pressures facing families, public services and neighbourhoods across Ealing. Her grassroots work spans campaigns for racial justice, the NHS and local libraries, as well as hands‑on initiatives such as food banks, clothes swaps and toddler sessions created in memory of her daughter. Debbie also supports Windrush families, serves as a school governor and contributes to the London Race Action Plan. She is committed to community‑led politics that protects residents, strengthens services and defends Ealing’s green spaces.

I remember the pride I felt when I first voted in an election, my vote was for the LibDems on the promise of not increasing tuition fees. A life lesson was not all I gained, a student debt now worth double what I borrowed and a 9% “tax” on my salary is the end result, and I am not alone. My parents couldn’t afford to send me to university and while thankful for the education I was afforded, I’m saddened by the fashion with which I had to agree to it. Had I only been born a year earlier my debt would be 1/3 what it is!

The establishment are letting us down and not working for people like us. Unlike other parties, the Green Party have a simple message of environmental and social justice and a rhetoric that things can be different and we should be hopeful not hateful.

I promise to fight for you every day, listening to your struggles and working with you and the council to find solutions. A renter all my adult life, I know the exceptional cost of living, difficulties saving for a deposit and the inequality and often poor living conditions that leads to. In such a large and crowded city, with many living in flats, the green spaces we are fortunate to have are vital for us. I will ensure they are clean and safe so we can all enjoy the outdoors.

I am a scientist working on technologies to treat drinking water, meaning I am data led and make informed decisions, see past the bluster and analyse critically. I will hold the council accountable and ensure they are delivering for us all.

I am a local resident who cares deeply about our community, our green spaces, and the people who live here. I grew up in an ecologically minded home where I learned the importance of protecting nature, reducing waste, and looking after the world we share. Those values have shaped my life and are a big part of why I am standing as a Green Party candidate.

I was previously involved in helping to run a grassroots activist group called Mothers Rise Up, where I worked with others to campaign on environmental and social issues affecting families and our future. I believe local action can make a real difference, and that communities are strongest when people feel connected, supported, and heard.

I am actively involved in local life through choirs, conservation activities, and teaching yoga, all of which help bring people together and support wellbeing. I want to help build a greener, fairer, and more caring community for everyone who lives here.

Sarah McCartney was born in Saltburn, Cleveland, and grew up in a making and repairing household in the North East of England. She was educated at Boldon Comprehensive School, Durham and West London Universities, and now lives in Ealing. Sarah managed the Guardian and Observer's events, was Lush Cosmetics' writer for 14 years, and was a regular cos- metics industry speaker on ethical issues. In 2011 she found- ed 4160 Tuesdays, an eco award winning craft perfumery now based in Hammersmith.

Tina has lived in the Borough of Ealing for almost 44 years and in Pitshanger since 1988. She was a secondary school teacher in Ealing and surrounding boroughs before retiring. For many years, she volunteered for the Pitshanger Community Association at events such as Party in the Park, mainly in charge of recycling.  She also introduced and has organised the popular annual charity PCA  Street Wars Quiz for the past 18 years. In addition she has volunteered for Ealing Food Bank and has tutored many students who have struggled at school.

Tina loves this borough and believes passionately in the protection of our urban wildlife and our environment. She joined ECI as she feels it is time for a change to community-based local politics.

In 2025, I was elected to serve on the Green Party’s Policy Development Committee, where I work to develop practical, forward-looking policies. I am an enthusiastic academic, campaigner, and policy developer dedicated to making a positive impact. My education and research at leading UK universities including Oxford and Cambridge has focused on how institutions can become more accountable, effective, and responsive to public needs.

Ealing has been my home for 15 years, I understand the challenges our residents face, from rising rents and housing costs to overdevelopment without sufficient infrastructure, air and water quality concerns, stretched local services, and limited opportunities for our young people.

Many people feel the system is not working for them. Too often, decisions are made without proper scrutiny or meaningful consultation. Residents want genuinely affordable housing, clean streets, maintained green spaces, safe neighbourhoods, well-supported schools, and transparency in how taxpayers’ money is spent. Managing decline is not leadership. We need ambition and accountability.

In 2022, the Green Party came second in this ward. Since then, we have grown strongly because more people recognise that real change is needed. We are now the credible alternative for those seeking hope, integrity, long-term thinking, and practical solutions.

If we want meaningful change in Ealing, we need Green councillors demanding social justice, environmental responsibility and prioritising people.

I will bring openness, scrutiny and evidence-based decision-making to South Acton and contribute to the wider Ealing alongside other Green elected councillors.

I will listen carefully, and work tirelessly to make sure that local decisions genuinely reflect what our community needs.

Please support me in South Acton and kindly encourage neighbours across Ealing to back Green candidates. Every conversation is valuable, and every vote truly makes a difference.

As your councillor,

I’ve been a member of the Green Party for 36 years. I’ve lived in Ealing for all that time and I’m now retired from the BBC where I was a technician.
I spent 15 years on the Independent Monitoring Board at Wormwood Scrubs prison. Crime and how we punish it is a particular area of interest to me.
I like hands-on, practical solution;, I litter pick, organised a street sweep when wet leaves were a danger and I work with Abundance to garden small (and not so small) public spaces that otherwise look shabby.
The ability of the public to communicate efficiently with the council is an area I’d really like to get to grips with!

Miça is standing for election to advocate for South Acton residents who for too long have been let down and marginalised by people in power. A keen socialist and activist, Miça has worked as a mental health nurse in the NHS for nearly 10 years and knows first-hand how our public services are being destroyed by relentless cuts. Miça has been part of Health Workers for Palestine and strongly supports calls for Ealing Council to divest from companies linked to the genocide in Palestine.

Miça has been a vocal opponent of cuts to Ealing’s Children’s Centres, and has worked tirelessly with West London Stand Up To Racism helping to mobilise against the far right in Brentford and West Drayton.

In South Acton, Miça is helping build a campaign to stop the Michael Flanders Centre being closed and to fight for better housing for all.

Miça is now focusing full time on activism and her rugby career – previously playing for Saracens, now for Trailfinders in Ealing, she hopes to bring that same fight and discipline as an athlete to her political work in Ealing Council.

Elizabeth joined Ealing Community Independents to protect public spaces and essential services from being lost to development or outsourcing. She has seen how the closure or sell‑off of children’s centres, adult care facilities (e.g. Michael Flanders Centre, Acton) and other community assets can leave residents without support. Elizabeth previously worked in the NHS and at Birkbeck, where she earned a PhD in cultural studies. Having lived in South Acton and returned over a decade ago, she is committed to ensuring public land, libraries, parks and social care remain in community use. Her goal is simple: a council that puts residents and public services first.

Glen has supported South Acton residents for over a decade through the Redbrick community organisation, helping neighbours navigate regeneration, repairs and local issues. During the Covid pandemic, he worked with Acton Gardens, Acton Youth Centre and local groups to deliver large‑scale emergency food support, leading to his recognition as a Community Champion. Glen also serves on Ealing’s Your Voice Your Town committee, helping fund projects that improve Acton’s environment and community spaces. Having lived and worked locally for many years, he is committed to strengthening community networks and ensuring residents have a meaningful voice in local decisions.

Angela has lived in Southall for twenty years and worked in the London Borough of Ealing at West London College for eighteen years. Angela has campaigned on air pollution firstly establishing Insistent Residents Against Toxic Emissions (IRATE) followed by Clean Air for Southall and Hayes (CASH). She is passionate about raising awareness of injustices affecting residents’ quality of life and has organised children’s play activities with other volunteers. Angela currently works as a teaching support in a local college, assisting students with learning and physical disabilities.

A Southall resident for 26 years, Qamer raised his family here and built strong relationships across our community. He’s campaigned on key local issues and seen first‑hand how Southall has been neglected, with persistent fly‑tipping, anti‑social behaviour, rising crime, poor roads and too few facilities for children and older residents. He’s standing to demand accountability and ensure Southall gets the respect and investment it deserves. If elected, he will be a dedicated voice for residents, working to make our area clean, safe, vibrant and welcoming for everyone who calls Southall home.

People before politics.
Joginder has been living in Southall since 1965 with his wife and two daughters, who have all grown up, married and are living locally as well. Joginder, known by his friends as Joe - has been running a road transport business since the late 80s. He got involved in local politics from the sidelines and used to attend many ward forums held by the council. Many residents ask Joe for guidance and advice on local problems, such as hospital departments that were closing, the parking problems faced by many residents or concerns about the construction of thousands of flats at the old gas works site.

Minni arrived in Southall as a three‑year‑old immigrant and has spent her entire life rooted in our community. Her career was based at Dogra & Co. Solicitors on Southall Green. She served as a school governor for 17 years.

After retiring, she retrained to teach English as a second language. But walking through Southall after the pandemic, she saw the decline caused by decisions made at Ealing Council. Minni began challenging these failures through letters, meetings, Freedom of Information requests, protests, and petitions on issues including overdevelopment, planning failures, broken pavements, the loss of ward forums, and cuts to vital public services.

Minni helped secure a public inquiry into the proposed Dominion car park towers, fought to save Southall Youth Centre, delivered a 4000‑signature petition to Downing Street to protect our Crown Post Office, opposed harmful CPZ extensions, and raised awareness of the proposed closure of half of Southall’s Children’s Centres – now subject to judicial review.

She understands Southall because she grew up in its story. Her commitment is simple: to give our community a strong voice and put Southall first.

Jatinder has lived in Southall his whole life and is deeply rooted in the community. With over 15 years of experience in creative leadership and campaign strategy, he has led national and global communications projects, bringing people together and driving change.

He is standing up to be a voice for those without one and will fight for better services and stronger community spaces for residents, local businesses, and the vulnerable. He wants to restore community pride and secure a better future for our children.

From our WWII veterans who gave everything to those who fought racism and the National Front in the 80s, their struggle was not in vain. Stand with him and shape Southall’s future.

Sukhi is a 49-year-old independent candidate committed to practical solutions that put our community first.

As a working professional and family man, he understands the real pressures people face, from rising living costs to the need for safer streets and stronger local services.

He believes in transparency, accountability, and responsible budgeting.

His focus is on improving community wellbeing, supporting local businesses, promoting healthy lifestyles, and ensuring decisions are made with residents – not political parties at the centre.

Sukhi is running to bring common sense, integrity, and genuine representation back to local government.

Dan moved to Southall in 2021 after living and working across more than 40 countries. With a background spanning retail, construction, insurance, tourism, entertainment and tech, he brings practical experience and a hands‑on approach to community problem‑solving. Since settling in the Green Quarter, he has joined several local groups focused on improving the area and strengthening resident involvement. Dan has been motivated by concerns about overdevelopment, stalled infrastructure and deteriorating streets. His priorities include ensuring developers deliver essential community facilities upfront and restoring pride in Southall through initiatives that improve neighbourhood upkeep and support a stronger, more connected community.

I’ve always cared somewhat about the natural world, and then I realised our environment is our life support system and needs to be regenerated so people can live full, healthy and happy lives. Therefore I joined the Green Party as they are the only ones prioritising our health and economic competitiveness.
Only the Greens have ambitious plans for education, which is the most efficient way to create real equality in our society, and a stronger economy. This, on top of building high quality low energy homes to permanently reduce the cost of living, means the Green Party is focussed on helping people to thrive, instead of just getting by.

I grew up in Wigan and studied Genetics at Sheffield University before moving to London where I have lives in West London for over twenty years. I love mountaineering and cooking. I work in data roles and have spent time analysing data for the hospitality sector within marketing departments and also worked in market research.

I became a Lib Dem councillor in 2002 and did so after wanting to help solve problems rather than just blame the council for their mistakes. My greatest achievement was a group effort linking the Liberal Democrats with campaigners so we could save Warren Farm from being developed in a way that would kill off around 20 endangered species of birds, animals, bugs and plants. After the long running campaign where there was a rally with over 2000 people the Council back tracked!

I have lived in this part of west London for forty years, moving to the Hounslow part of Chiswick in 1984. My wife and I moved to Ealing twenty years later. My work career has been largely retail and/or book related. Currently I work for an international charity and manage a part time a second hand bookshop.

First elected in 2010 for Southfield Ward, after standing as a candidate previously in Ealing and Hounslow. For the past fourteen years, local government has been through hard times financially, despite having increased responsibilities. Sadly that is not likely to change anytime soon. But being a Councillor is interesting and one still gets a buzz from helping residents, especially if you can make a genuine difference.

I have lived in Ealing for just over 3 years and have always loved the diversity of the borough, both of the environment and also of the community.

I am passionate about human rights and have volunteered in my spare time with refugees and asylum seekers for the last ten years.

I joined the Green Party because I found hope in its values after years of watching and feeling our society being let down time and time again by the government. It’s time for a return to basics – what does this community need to thrive? How can everyone feel a part of it? And how can we make it happen?
